panelReasoning: Unanimous 4/4 for B. With the root at height 0 the tree has levels 0..k, level i holds at most 2^i nodes, so max nodes = sum_{i=0..k} 2^i = 2^(k+1) - 1 (two judges computed it directly and by the recurrence m(h) = 1 + 2*m(h-1): k=0 -> 1, k=1 -> 3, k=2 -> 7, k=3 -> 15). Every other option dies on arithmetic: A/D '2^k - 1' gives 0 nodes at k=0, impossible; C '2^(k-1) + 1' gives 2 at k=1 where the true max is 3. The trap is the off-by-one: candidates recall '2^h - 1', which is valid only when the root is counted as level 1. The source-archaeologist settled the duplicate-option question by going to the paper itself ('Test 3/IMG_9100.HEIC', page 3): options A and D really are printed identically as '2^k - 1', so the duplication is a defect in the original exam, not a pipeline garble, and no superscript was lost in extraction. This overrides the skeptic's external-bank guess that the printed D was '2^k + 1'. The printed answer key ('Test 3/IMG_9111.HEIC', row 10) gives Ans = B with the same explanation the item ships. Source key, printed paper and mathematics all agree, and the duplicate lands on two wrong options, so key uniqueness is untouched and the item is answerable exactly as shipped.

3/4 for D (first-principles 0.78, distractor-analysis 0.72, source-archaeologist 0.90); the skeptic voted unanswerable at 0.80. Quarantine is not triggered: it requires the skeptic's unanswerable AND another judge below 0.6, and the lowest agreeing confidence is 0.72. Ships as D, badged key_disputed. Facts established: the true worst case of binary search is floor(log2 n) + 1 comparisons, i.e. Theta(log n) (instrumented and counted: n=1024 -> 11, n=1048576 -> 21 comparisons, not 1048576). O(log n) is NOT among the printed options, and the source-archaeologist confirmed on the paper itself ('Test 3/IMG_9102.HEIC', page 5) that the printed option list is exactly O(n^2) / O(n log n) / O(n^3) / O(n) - nothing was dropped by extraction. The printed key ('Test 3/IMG_9112.HEIC', row 24, and again at Q26 on the same page) reads Ans = D. So the item is intrinsically defective, but D remains the only defensible bubble: big-O is an upper bound, log n is in O(n) as well as in all three looser options, and O(n) is the tightest bound offered. The shipped explanation ('binary search will be left or right intended, making it compare all the n values') is false for binary search on a sorted array; it is the worst-case argument for search in a SKEWED BINARY SEARCH TREE, which is almost certainly the question the author meant to print. Shade D as scored; memorise the fact 'binary search worst case = O(log n)', not the paper's reasoning. Repair suggestion: replace one distractor with O(log n), or retire the item.

finalAnswer: Table (the source key writes 'table or file')
panelReasoning: Unanimous 4/4 on 'table', and the source-archaeologist upgraded it from a derivation to a recovered key. Extraction was faithful: in 'computer engineering 2016 - extra.docx' the item is a bare numbered line 'Name of relation in Data base?' with no options and no trailing '**' answer marker (the marker convention that file uses elsewhere, e.g. 'd) Record **'), which is exactly why the pipeline recorded an empty key. The key does exist outside that docx: the tutor answer-key PDF '/Users/meshal/Downloads/Telegram Desktop/Data.pdf' (cover 'KNPC / Subject 3 Computer science / Part (2)', footer 'ENG. MOHANNAD'), page 18, reproduces this stem with the highlighted answer 'table or file', on the same page as the sibling items 'Name of a row in a database? d) Record' and 'How to delete a table in a database? a) Drop', which pins it to this 2016 set. That matches the theory the other three judges derived independently (Codd / Elmasri: a relation is realised as a TABLE; tuples = rows/records, attributes = columns/fields) and matches the examiner's established pattern in sibling knpc2016-q008 (row -> Record), which asks for the everyday database name of a relational-model concept rather than the formal term. The reverse reading ('what is a table called in the relational model' -> 'relation'), which capped two judges at 0.55, is ruled out by the recovered key. Stem left as printed since the extraction is faithful to the source; it is a garbled recall fragment and could be reworded to 'In a relational database, a relation is also known as a ___' if a stem repair pass is run.

Unanimous 4/4 for B (Software Stability). No key exists anywhere: the source-archaeologist rendered the recall paper ('KNPC 2021 EXAM (1).pdf' p.4, byte-identical in 'KNPC 2021 EXAM (3).pdf', and as p.42 of 'KOC 2012 EXAM (2).pdf'), which marks its answers with bold+underline (visible on Q10, Q17, Q18, Q19 on the same and facing pages) but leaves Q12-Q15 unmarked, and a full-text grep plus OCR of every exam and tutor PDF in the corpus returns no occurrence of 'stability'/'durability'/'keeps changing' outside those unmarked copies. So the key genuinely never existed and nothing was suppressed by extraction. Decided by definition-matching: Stability is the attribute defined as the degree to which requirements and the software do NOT change over time (IEEE 830 requires an SRS be 'ranked for importance and stability'; requirements stability is measured by the number of changes to a requirement), so software that keeps changing during development is exhibiting a deficit of stability. Every distractor fails on subject matter, not on nuance: Consistency = absence of contradiction among requirements at a point in time; Durability = the D of ACID in transaction processing, a vocabulary decoy imported from databases and not a software-quality attribute at all; Testability = the degree to which the software admits test criteria. B is the only option whose definition is about change over time. Corroborating: the neighbouring item knpc2021-q012 in the same recall set also offers Consistency and Stability, showing the paper deliberately drills that distinction. The stem is a truncated fragment ('When a software keeps changing during its development'), but every plausible completion ('...it lacks ___', '...this concerns which attribute?') lands on Stability, so the item is answerable; it is the item's grammar, not its key, that needs repair.

3/4 for A (first-principles 0.85, distractor-analysis 0.80, skeptic 0.75); the source-archaeologist reported D at 0.45 and explicitly asked not to let it outvote a well-argued A. Ships as A, badged key_disputed. The archaeologist-wins rule does NOT fire here, because they did not find the original paper saying something different: they rendered the recall paper ('KNPC 2021 EXAM (1).pdf' p.4 / 'KOC 2012 EXAM (2).pdf' p.42) and confirmed Q14 is printed with NO answer marking at all, i.e. the pipeline's empty key is a faithful extraction. Their D comes from a third-party tutor key ('/Users/meshal/Downloads/Telegram Desktop/Data.pdf' p.18, footer 'ENG. MOHANNAD'), where option D is yellow-highlighted; that is a tutor's opinion, not the exam's key, and it is contradicted by standard theory. Theory is unambiguous: in the ER model a relationship set has no key of its own, and the union of the primary keys of the participating entity sets is a superkey of the relationship set (Silberschatz, 'Primary key for relationship sets'; Elmasri/Navathe: a relationship instance is identified by the participating entity instances). This is exactly why the M:N junction table's primary key is the composite of the participants' foreign keys. B ('primary key of the relation itself') is the designed trap, exploiting the relation/relationship word collision: the junction table's PK is itself the concatenation of the participants' PKs, so B collapses into A and cannot be a distinct answer. C fails because descriptive attributes of a relationship (e.g. a date on Works_On) are non-identifying: two different employee-project pairs can share them. D, the tutor's pick, is flatly false and is refuted by the existence of that composite key. A is the textbook answer and the answer to the standard version of this MCQ. Answer A, badged so the user knows the only physical key in the corpus disagrees; the tutor is most likely wrong.

Unanimous 4/4 for A, and the source-archaeologist recovered a physical key that agrees. The recall paper ('KNPC 2021 EXAM (1).pdf' pp.4-5 / 'KOC 2012 EXAM (2).pdf' pp.42-43) prints Q15 split across a page break with all four options unmarked, so the pipeline's empty key was a faithful extraction. The tutor answer-key PDF '/Users/meshal/Downloads/Telegram Desktop/Data.pdf' page 18 reproduces the item and yellow-highlights option A ('Primary key of a participating relation'), verified at 400dpi with B/C/D unhighlighted; the highlighter is not off-by-one, since the adjacent item's highlight sits on its own distinct option. Theory agrees (Silberschatz, Relational Model): 'a relation schema r1 may include among its attributes the primary key of ANOTHER relation schema r2. This attribute is called a foreign key from r1, referencing r2' - the defining content is a primary key of a different, participating/referenced relation, which is what enforces referential integrity. B (Super key) is an intra-relation identifier, the opposite of an inter-relation reference. C (Composite key) describes arity, not role: a foreign key may be single-attribute. The item's real weakness is the near-duplicate pair A vs D, a recall-degradation artefact, but they are not semantically identical and the discriminating word decides it: D ('Primary key of a relation') strips out the referencing half and merely restates an ordinary primary key - under D every primary key would be a foreign key, which is absurd - so D is the classic trap. 'Participating' is this examiner's own word for the other party in a reference, as the immediately preceding item q014 shows. Source key and theory both give A. Non-blocking repair suggestion: reword D, or expand A to 'primary key of another, referenced relation'.
